posts.caption:
In the aftermath of heartbreak, London-based photographer @alexandrawaespi was drawn to Tokyo as a place that dignifies solitude. ⁠
⁠
“It’s a place that allows you to be alone easily; it almost encourages it,” she tells @ohnoitsemilyd. “I like being alone and getting lost.” It’s also a city that harbours a particularly fascinating network of trends and subcultures. It was through a visit to Nail Sucre in Shinjuku – one of the city’s many extraordinary nail art shops – that Waespi encountered the arresting style and ideology of gyaru, with its “dramatic nails, exaggerated makeup, dyed hair, layered accessories, anime and game influences”. Captivated, she began an ongoing photo project, gyaru*, which would deepen her relationship with Tokyo.⁠
⁠
Read Emily Dinsdale's interview with Alex Waespi at the link in bio 📲⁠
⁠
📸 gyaru* by @alexandrawaespi⁠

posts.caption:
@wintervandenbrink first met the @idea.ltd team three years ago at a book signing. Six months later, co-founder and photographer, Angela Hill invited him for a drink at her Paris hotel. “She mentioned we should do a book,” he says. The result was Vandals (2024), a 400-page doorstopper of unwitting youth portraits. Two years on, Wolves expands on this logic, turning the lens to the singular within the collective: still, watchful, almost eerily cool. ⁠
⁠
“When I’m shooting in public, it’s kind of like hunting and meditating at the same time,” Vandenbrink reveals. The images were shot across various European cities, Amsterdam, Paris, Milan, and Venice, some only months before printing. His previous work was made at a distance, anonymous and voyeuristic; for this project he’s submerged himself in the action.⁠
⁠
Read @w.lliam's interview with Vandenbrink at the link in bio 📲⁠
⁠
📸 Wolves. Photography by Winter Vandenbrink. Courtesy of IDEA⁠

posts.caption:
On the first day of @wimbledon, @naomiosaka stepped onto the court for her opening match against French player Jacquemot. While Wimbledon’s strict all-white dress code leaves little room for personal expression, Osaka still found a way to make it her own.
For the occasion, she wore a custom look by Tokyo-based designer @_hanayagi, inspired by the traditional Japanese kimono. The design featured embroidered crane and cherry blossom motifs, a ribbon sash tied at the waist, and a layered sheer skirt that created soft movement. Once the outer layer came off, her Nike match kit was revealed underneath, with floral appliqué details that tied the entire look together
Even within Wimbledon’s strict dress code, Osaka celebrated fashion to celebrate her Japanese heritage while pushing the boundaries of on-court style. Swipe to see all the details.
Words by Julie Jeong
Next-Level Fashion. Daily.
Follow @a2z_fashionmag Now.
📷 @wimbledon, @_hanayagi, Matthias Hangst/Getty Images
#Wimbledon #NaomiOsaka

posts.caption:
On 29 June, @marcjacobs SS27 show was held at the New York Public Library. The show lasted only four minutes and featured 31 looks, but it said everything.
This collection reflected #MarcJacobs’ classic aesthetic, featuring pop-colour tights such as neon yellow and pink, layered jewellery, contrasting materials like PVC and sheer, and minimal yet structured silhouettes.
One of the most notable elements of the Marc Jacobs collection was the makeup, and this season was no exception. With the recent relaunch of the @marcjacobsbeauty line, eye makeup and lip colours stood out throughout the show—either complementing or contrasting with each look. The makeup further amplified the mood of the collection, sharpening its overall impact.
In the show notes, @themarcjacobs revealed the references behind the collection, ranging from his own archives, including Marc Jacobs SS98 and @louisvuitton SS09, to @chanelofficial SS93 and Bob Fosse’s All That Jazz. Swipe right to see all the details.
Words by Julie Jeong
Next-Level Fashion. Daily.
Follow @a2z_fashionmag Now.
📷 @marcjacobs, JB Villareal / Shoot Digital, Alessandro Lucioni

posts.caption:
The Championships @wimbledon 2026 officially kick off today!
A week before the official start of Wimbledon, the editor was invited to attend the WTA (Women’s Tennis Association) cocktail party hosted by world No. 3 tennis player @AmandaAnisimova. Held at a Roof Gardens in London, the event was filled with an energy as intense as the midsummer heat. Exclusive cocktails created for the WTA, including the ‘Centre Court Spritz’ and ‘Match Point Paloma,’ alongside carefully curated finger foods, further elevated the atmosphere of the space.
As the party reached its peak, the editor had the opportunity to briefly meet Anisimova. Wearing a piece from @davidkomalondon SS26, she embodied a composed yet powerful presence, carrying a sense of unwavering confidence amid the pressure and competition on court—truly reflecting her status as one of the most closely watched players in women’s tennis today.
Swipe right to get a closer look at moments from the event.
Words by Julie Jeong
Next-Level Fashion. Daily.
Follow @a2z_fashionmag Now.
📷 @a2z_fashionmag
#Wimbledon #Championships
#AmandaAnisimova #WTA

posts.caption:
JW ANDERSON presents Spartacus, Bob Mizer’s Physique Pictorial at JW ANDERSON London Soho, in celebration of Pride Month.
Created by American photographer Bob Mizer in 1951, Physique Pictorial was a pioneering magazine dedicated to male physique photography. Through images of bodybuilders, models and athletic young men, the publication celebrated strength, beauty and desire at a time when queer visual culture was often hidden or heavily censored. For this exhibition, JW ANDERSON focuses on the anonymous artist known as “Spartacus,” whose illustrations brought a more openly homoerotic imagination to the male body.
The exhibition runs from 25 June to 6 July at JW ANDERSON SOHO, with artworks available to buy in store.
📌 Bob Mizer’s Physique Pictorial at JW ANDERSON SOHO
• Date: 25 June to 6 July
• Location: JW Anderson London Soho, 2 Brewer St, W1F 0SA
Words by Josephine Jiang
Next-Level Fashion, Daily.
Follow @a2z_fashionmag Now.
📷@a2z_fashionmag

posts.caption:
On 29 June, @jacquemus’ Le Bonheur collection took place in Corsica. Under the theme of ‘Happiness,’ the show unfolded in the place where @simon_porte_jacquemus felt his mum looked the happiest.
Inspired by the sunset tone along the South of France coastline and a palette drawn from the sea, Jacquemus’ signature exaggerated volumes felt more refined and precise this season. It moved between relaxed silhouettes and structured tailoring, creating looks that felt lighter - both for the wearer and for those watching. The sheer tops and dresses, and the way they were styled, feel like a quiet reference point for this summer’s holiday wardrobe.
The editor still hasn’t decided where to go for my summer holiday, but after watching the show, I kept thinking about the South of France, wearing a vibrant bikini under a sheer dress, wandering slowly along the beach, where nothing really happened. Just a long, easy holiday.
Words by Julie Jeong
Next-Level Fashion. Daily.
Follow @a2z_fashionmag Now.
📷 @jacquemus
#Jacquemus #LeBonheur
#SouthOfFrance #Corsica

On 28 June, @ateez_official_ performed as a headliner at @bsthydepark. To get a closer look at their performance, ATINY from across the UK gathered at the festival to experience the group’s powerful energy up close. Over approximately 1.5 hours, ATEEZ showcased a diverse range of performances, from ‘BOUNCY (K-HOT CHILLI PEPPERS)’ to their recently released track ‘Bad.’ The group appeared in all-white looks, with each member expressing their individuality through their styling.
For those who couldn’t attend the event in person, we’ve also prepared video footage captured on site through the editor’s lens. Swipe right to feel ATEEZ’s intense energy.
Words by Julie Jeong

posts.caption:
“Sometimes I love to overdo everything and go crazy. I love bright colours: pink, orange, sunset colours, or the colours of beautiful flowers,” says @inkawilliams as a self-described minimalist, with maximalist tendencies. The French-Australian model is a vision of effortless beauty as she embodies the youthful spirit of the Chanel Rouge Coco Hydra Gloss, its frosted finishes, vivid colours and high-shine, hypnotising tubes.
Through the link in bio, see the beauty behind Inka Williams’ salty skin, flushed cheeks and reflective lips.
#InkaWilliams wears @chanel.beauty Rouge Coco Hydra Gloss and Summer Makeup Collection on the June 2026 digital cover of Harper’s BAZAAR Australia/New Zealand.
Words: @itsalexjduffy
Photography: @jamie_.green
Makeup: @victoriabaron using @chanel.beauty
Hair: @darrensummors
Styling: @rachelwayman
Fashion assistant: @kaileeewaller
Production director: @rebeccamooreproductions
Art director: @_cathryn.s.z
Digital director: @ariellekatos
posts.alt_text:
While Bali is ingrained in @inkawilliams’ life and sense of self, the beach is something she had to leave to love. “My mum used to force us to go to the beach. I remember hating it. I’m a Virgo . . . all the sand on my hands and my feet when I would go back to the car,” she tells BAZAAR. “Eventually, when I grew up and moved to London, I realised how much I need the beach. I don’t mind if it’s cold, but I need the sun wherever I am. I need nature. If I go too long without being in nature, I start to lose my mind.”

At the link in bio, Inka Williams details her connection to Bali and the daily rituals which keep her grounded far from home. 

#InkaWilliams wears @chanel.beauty Rouge Coco Hydra Gloss and Summer Makeup Collection on the June 2026 digital cover of Harper’s BAZAAR Australia/New Zealand. 

Words: @itsalexjduffy
Videography: @jordansimpsonmotion
Makeup: @victoriabaron using @chanel.beauty
Hair: @darrensummors
Styling: @rachelwayman
Fashion assistant: @kaileeewaller

Digital director: @ariellekatos

posts.caption:
When home is somewhere between the island of Bali, Melbourne, Los Angeles, London and Paris, ‘home’ is a feeling @inkawilliams finds within herself. The Australian-born model was raised in Bali, where her childhood home remains an oasis from bustling city streets and long days on set. “I just love being at home,” says Williams. “The garden is just so beautiful. I feel like I’m living in a painting. Everything [in Bali] has developed around the house, but my house is still a bubble of green. I never want to leave home.”
Through the link in bio, the model and creative multihyphenate shares more on feeling at home across the world and with her love.
#InkaWilliams wears @chanel.beauty Rouge Coco Hydra Gloss and Summer Makeup Collection on the June 2026 digital cover of Harper’s BAZAAR Australia/New Zealand.

The appearance also coincides with The Devil Wears Prada celebrating its 20th anniversary, making the moment feel all the more iconic.

Guided by the idea of lightness, organza, cotton, silk satin and delicate appliqué feathers drifted down the runway, each look moving as though carried by the Mediterranean breeze.

Double shirting — even triple, if you’re really committing — is officially having a moment. Spotted everywhere from street style to the FROW, layering button-downs is proving to be one of the season’s chicest styling tricks.
Add sculptural silhouettes, elevated accessories and a great knit into the mix, and you’ve got winter dressing sorted.
Scroll through for BAZAAR’s guide to shirting now, then head to the link in bio to shop the story.

posts.caption:
Harper’s Bazaar India digital cover star, Maye Musk (@mayemusk), started modelling as a teenager. She stopped at 22 to get married and raise three children, then picked it back up at 28—an age the industry considered, at the time, well past the point of no return. So she built her nutrition practice in parallel. By 31, she was in high demand as a model and continued part-time; in her 60s, she was doing high fashion. “If someone had told me as a teenager that I would be in Delhi for a top fashion magazine at 78, I would have thought they were crazy,” she says. “Yet, here I am, working alongside a very creative team.
Read the full cover story at the link in bio.

From discussing masculinity and mindset to family, fashion, and finding comfort in authenticity, Jasprit Bumrah (@jaspritb1) keeps it refreshingly real in conversation with digital editor Sonal Ved (@sonalved). Speaking about everything from his love for fragrances and watches to switching off with family time, the ace cricketer proves that personal style is less about trends and more about individuality. He also shares why collaborating with Ralph Lauren Fragrances (@ralphlaurenfragrances) felt like a natural fit for his personal style and personality. Whether on the field or off duty, his approach remains grounded, thoughtful and deeply personal.

Tap the link in bio to watch the full video.

Styling: @eshaamiin1 

#BazaarIndia #HarpersBazaarIndia

posts.caption:
In an industry where surnames are inherited currencies, Bazaar India’s April-May cover star, Tabu (@tabutiful), has operated for over three decades on a mononym. To understand her career is to understand what it meant to say yes to things the industry was not ready for and to have the instinct to recognise those things as worthy, even when no template existed for them. But she refuses to call it rebellion. “It was simply a reflection of who I am. The roles I chose, the decisions I made—they came from some similar place throughout my life. Some part of me has been completely unchanged,” she insists.
Read the full cover story at the link in bio.
Editor: Rasna Bhasin (@rasnabhasin)

posts.alt_text:
Harper’s Bazaar India digital cover star, Maye Musk (@mayemusk), started modelling as a teenager. She stopped at 22 to get married and raise three children, then picked it back up at 28—an age the industry considered, at the time, well past the point of no return. So she built her nutrition practice in parallel. By 31, she was in high demand as a model and continued part-time; in her 60s, she was doing high fashion. “If someone had told me as a teenager that I would be in Delhi for a top fashion magazine at 78, I would have thought they were crazy,” she says. “Yet, here I am, working alongside a very creative team. 

Read the full cover story at the link in bio.

posts.caption:
Work is no longer defined by how high you climb, but by why you choose to stay where you are. ‘Conscious unbossing’ is reshaping the idea of ambition at work, as more professionals question the pressure to move into management roles. Many are stepping back from leadership tracks to prioritise work that feels more meaningful and sustainable, signalling a shift in how success is understood. Instead of climbing the hierarchy at all costs, work is being re-evaluated through fulfilment and personal well-being.
Tap the link in bio to read on.
✍️: Tanisha Saxena
📸: IMDb
#BazaarIndia #HarpersBazaarIndia

Polka dots are one fashion staple you can never go wrong with. Whether it’s a chic dinner, a glamorous soirée, or a playful daytime outing, the beloved print always rises to the occasion. This season, A-listers like Khushi Kapoor (@khushikapoor) and Demi Moore (@demimoore) are embracing polka dots in all their glory, from timeless black-and-white ensembles to fresh interpretations featuring playful pops of pink and blue. Classic yet endlessly versatile, the print continues to prove why it deserves a place in every well-dressed wardrobe.

posts.caption:
In the space of just a few seasons — since 41-year-old design whiz Matthieu Blazy staged his debut show at Paris’ Grand Palais in October 2025 — luxury stalwart Chanel has become one of fashion’s hottest brands.
Bruno Pavlovsky, president of Chanel’s fashion division since 2004 and a key player in its turnaround, carefully orchestrated Blazy’s onboarding as well as the successful rollout of his debut collection to stores.
In the latest edition of our State of Fashion series, Pavlovsky explains how the French couture giant has restored customer excitement — and sales momentum — by carefully balancing continuity and reinvention.
Discover more insights in Face to Face With Luxury Clients, the latest report from BoF Insights and McKinsey & Company #linkinbio
✍️ Robert Williams (@r.o.bertwilliams)
📷 @ChanelOfficial
posts.alt_text:
The suits showed up in force, and they were early.

A few minutes before 7 p.m. on Monday, before the doors were even open, Yehuda Shmidman, the founder, chairman and CEO of the brand management firm WHP Global, was whisked into the Fifth Avenue entrance of the New York Public Library, where Marc Jacobs was showing his first runway collection since LVMH announced the sale of his company to WHP and operating partner G-III Apparel Group in May.

The grand lobby inside the main branch of the library looked like it often does during a Marc Jacobs show, as fashion legends Mario Sorrenti, Grace Coddington, Phyllis Posnick, Anna Sui and others mingled on a hot summer night, but with the added element of the executives, who at one point included Shmidman, G-III chairman Morris Goldfarb and Eric Marechalle, whom LVMH appointed CEO of Marc Jacobs nearly a decade ago, all in a scrum surrounding Anna Wintour. LVMH veteran Sidney Toledano, a longtime champion of Jacobs, was there too. Toledano said he flew in from Paris Monday morning and was flying back Monday night. “I move at the speed of light,” the executive said.

There’s a lot of curiosity about the sale of Marc Jacobs, after 29 years with LVMH (more than half of them as the designer who made Louis Vuitton a luxury fashion behemoth), and how the brand’s new owners, once the transaction closes, will manage a designer who is one of the most influential in American fashion and yet whose runway offering isn’t as visible as it once was.

Read the full report by Eric Wilson (@ericwilsonsays) #linkinbio 

📷 Spotlight/Launchmetrics.com
🎥 @kittykewlcat

posts.caption:
Valentino Beauty, a brand under L’Oréal’s Luxe division, has appointed Dakota Johnson and Alexander Skarsgård as its new global ambassadors, The Business of Beauty can exclusively reveal.
Johnson, who already serves as a fashion ambassador for Maison Valentino, and Skarsgård will star in an upcoming global campaign for a yet-to-be-revealed Valentino fragrance. The pair were selected for their complementary personalities and natural chemistry, said Claudia Marcocci, global president of Valentino Beauty.
Read more on the house's latest appointment at the #linkinbio
✍️ @rachaelgriffs
📷 @valentino.beauty
posts.alt_text:
Pañpuri, the Thai purveyor of perfume oils and other fine bath products, is making two big bets. 

The first is on its retail fleet, which the Kosé-owned brand plans to expand by 16 locations across China, Japan and elsewhere in North Asia. But its second, more audacious bid is to become T-beauty’s first international success story. 

BoF's Rachael Griffiths (@rachaelgriffs) spoke with Pañpuri’s CEO Vorravit Siripark about the brand’s expansion and T-beauty’s global proposition. Read more #linkinbio

📷 @panpuriofficial

posts.caption:
A survey of more than 2,000 luxury clients across the US and China reveals that emotional connection sits at the centre of brand desirability today — but how that connection is established differs between the world's two most important luxury markets.
That is the central finding of "Face to Face With Luxury Clients," a new research report released today in our State of Fashion series produced by BoF Insights, The Business of Fashion's data and advisory team, in collaboration with @mckinseyco.
The report draws on a wide-ranging survey spanning luxury client spending tiers in the US and China, combined with in-depth interviews with customers ranging from aspirational to ultra-high-net-worth individuals, and executive interviews at Chanel, Jessica McCormack, Urban Jürgensen and ICICLE. It offers a detailed account of what luxury clients in these two markets want — uncovering a fundamental shift in priorities that is reshaping the path to growth for brands across the sector.
Download the special luxury edition of The State of Fashion to understand what really matters to luxury clients today. #linkinbio

posts.caption: boy love and girl love series are insanely loved in asia by young and old straight and gay. . in Bangkok, ads in metros, skytrains, airports etcetera show young men in full eye make up, lace tops, pearls and pink lip gloss selling toothpaste or cat food. it’s quite mainstream out of the workplace,of course, this acceptance of a different way to be a man, straight or gay, in regards to style. i see the Dries silky low cut tops being styled under blazers or moto jackets by k pop boy bands. young men will break down all these looks and style them as they want, not necessarily wearing the runway looks but buying the commercial pieces. something for everyone 🤓

posts.alt_text:
Our cover star opts for cinema that sparks conversations, takes pride in her individuality, and fiercely protects her mental peace. “Today, there’s clarity in my choices. I’m shedding old versions of myself to make space for newer, riskier, more interesting ones,” she says, unfiltered, unassuming and refreshingly candid. Bhumi Satish Pednekkar welcomes Brides Today (@bhumisatishpednekkar) into her world of stylish experiments, socially resonant movies, sustainable choices, and the legacy she hopes to leave behind. 

Check out the complete cover story in the link in bio.

posts.caption:
"In learning how to better recognize abundance, even in life's most uncertain moments, amidst challenge lay purpose and through change lay possibility, creating as an act of gratitude is my truest form of self-expression," wrote @marcjacobs in the show notes that accompanied his Spring 2027 runway presentation on Monday at the New York Public Library.
The blink-and-you'd-miss-it show (which lasted just four minutes) featured the designer's creative interpretation of the theme of gratitude. Sheer lamé dresses, hot pink croc mini skirts, sequin embroidered blazers and pantyhose in every color of the rainbow were all incorporated across the new collection. Swipe through for a peek at some of the standout looks.
📸: Rachel Tashjian/CNN; Dimitrios Kambouris/Getty Images
posts.alt_text:
Heatwaves are by nature an anomaly and the collections shown during fashion week are designed months in advance. But Europe is now the fastest-warming continent on Earth, heating up at a rate of twice the global average. The UN predicts the next 5 years will break even more temperature records.

Should designers, who work in an industry that is one of the biggest culprits for greenhouse gas emissions, at least be making clothes that people could reasonably aspire to wear in the hellfire summers to come? Or should we just submit to the heat-induced reverie?

Swipe to see looks from a sweltering Men’s Fashion Week in Paris, and read the full story at link in bio.

posts.caption:
Barbie was Martin Margiela’s first model. Some of his first designs were for the plastic fashionista. Shortly after he launched his brand in 1988, he was asked to recreate three ensembles from his Fall 1989 show, down to the shiny industrial tape belts and crackle-painted Tabi boots, for Barbies. After they were shown, though, the dolls were lost. “I was in shock,” he writes of the experience. “I think I never could cope with that loss.” During the pandemic, more than 30 years later, he realized he still had the patterns and remade them. Now, they’re up for auction along with his personal archive of designs, prototypes, memorabilia and more at Paris’s Maurice Auction in July.
Fashion is so alienating, especially to those of us on its fringes. “Avant-garde” clothes often arrive on unsmiling models, peddled by self-serious designers. Maybe Margiela’s greatest contribution to fashion, or the lesson designers and observers should take away, is that he had none of that severity, and so much tender mischief.
If you’re seeking more under-the-radar industry offerings, subscribe to weekly sends of our newsletter Big Style. You can get weekly emails from CNN senior style reporter and advanced outfit anthropologist @theprophetpizza, excavating stylish amusements and contextualizing the world of fashion right in your inbox. Sign up at the link in bio or comment “Big Style” and we’ll hook you up.
📸: Marc Chatelard courtesy of Maurice Auction

posts.caption:
While soccer is the most popular sport in the world, its position in the US has been more of a "slow acceptance," said fashion photographer Phil Oh.
Ten years ago, there were certain stereotypes around wearing jerseys, Oh said — hipsters calling the game "footy," for example, a try-hard association inspiring eye rolls.
But as soccer has grown more popular stateside, so has the jersey.
In the early 2010s, @snoopdogg became known for incorporating a variety of soccer jerseys into his wardrobe, even those of warring teams, drawing some side-eyes from die-hard fans. @champagnepapi wore a pink juventus away jersey in 2016, a move seemingly so rare for a rapper of his stature that eBay listings still sell the jersey as a "Juventus x Drake."
It was around that time that Josh Warwick, one of the co-founders of @cultkits, a vintage soccer jersey retail website, began noticing a shift. The jerseys were being bought by people who weren't just soccer nerds or fans looking for deep cuts.
The jersey trend in the US has grown so much that in the last two to three years, the US has surpassed the UK to become Cult Kits' biggest market. And the ubiquity with which the soccer jersey has been adopted by various brands signals growing demand.
📸  Brian Rasic/WireImage/Getty Images/From Spotify/Melodie Jeng/NOWFASHION/Shutterstock/Claudio Lavenia/Edward Berthelot

posts.caption:
Whether found in a respected museum or a billionaire's mansion, it is likely that any given Khmer sculpture in the West was, at some point, ripped from an ancient temple complex and trafficked out of Cambodia. There is also a reasonable chance it passed through the hands of a British man called Douglas Latchford.
To his customers, the antiquities dealer was a respectable figure — a trusted vendor, prominent art scholar and author of multiple books on sculpture from the Khmer Empire, a civilization that prospered in what is now Cambodia and other parts of Southeast Asia between the 9th and 15th centuries. From the 1960s until his death in 2020, Latchford supplied collectors with ornate friezes, temple carvings and statues of Hindu gods, Buddhas and bodhisattvas.
It now appears that much of Latchford's inventory had been illegally pillaged from abandoned archeological sites like Angkor Wat and Koh Ker.
Small-scale looters, sometimes with the help of local military personnel, would remove the works with shovels, chisels, picks and even dynamite before transporting them, often by oxcart, to the Thai border. The items then found their way to the Bangkok-based dealer who, while now long dead, stands accused of laundering them onto the global art market using falsified records.
Some of the artifacts later appeared at major auction houses or joined the collections of museums including New York's Metropolitan Museum of Art.
📸: Illustration by Leah Abucayan/CNN/Reuters/Getty/Sipa/Cambodian Ministry of Culture and Fine Arts

Check out his striking latest collection, “Ouro Verde,” which celebrates traditional healing practices and African spirituality #cnnafricanvoices
📷 images courtesy of: 1-4, 6, 8-9: @filafriques /Alexis Peskine, 5, 7: Alexis Peskine
posts.alt_text:
Once common in coastal waters worldwide, guitarfish are now among the ocean's most threatened fish, as demand for their fins fuels a multimillion-dollar trade. In Ghana, marine biologist Dr. Issah Seidu is protecting these 'living fossils' by working with the same fishers who used to catch them #calltoearth 

Click on the link in our bio for more.

📷 images courtesy of: 1,2,5,7,8,9,10: Issah Seidu; 3: Olympia de Maismont/AFP/Getty Images; 4: Johan Fredriksson/Wikimedia Commons (CC BY-SA 3.0); 6: Antony Dickson/AFP/Getty Images

There are influencers, and then there’s @madelineargy. Since 2021, the “Pretty Lonesome” host has made eight million strangers feel like her closest friends without even trying. Whether she’s giving advice on toxic friendships or crashing out over Jane Goodall, it’s clear that Argy’s got range—and so does her closet.

Coveteur went inside the closet of her LA home and found rows of candy-colored @chanelofficial bags on one wall and thrifted tanks from @thredup and @depop on the other.

Tap the link in bio for our latest #ClosetTour to step inside her world, from the breakup that led to her most-treasured boots to why she’ll defend backpacks until the very end.

We caught up with Thomas backstage to hear more about the inspiration behind the look, created using @marcjacobsbeauty—swipe for more.

posts.caption:
Long before footballers became luxury brand ambassadors and carefully curated style icons, Mario Balotelli was dressing entirely on his own terms.
Throughout the late 2000s and early 2010s, his wardrobe reflected the same unpredictability that defined his career. Leather jackets, oversized fur, sharp tailoring, statement sunglasses, distressed denim, bold prints, and designer sneakers all became part of his signature look. It wasn’t about following trends, it was about standing out.
Balotelli embraced fashion with confidence and a sense of excess that felt unmistakably of its time. While many players leaned toward understated luxury, he preferred outfits that sparked conversation, often blurring the line between football star and pop culture personality.
Looking back, his style captures an era when fashion was louder, less calculated, and far more instinctive. Before “quiet luxury” became the industry’s obsession, Balotelli represented a different kind of menswear, one built on confidence, individuality, and the willingness to take risks.
Whether people loved it or hated it, one thing was undeniable: nobody dressed quite like Mario Balotelli.
posts.alt_text:
Between 1998 and 1999, Juergen Teller stood outside his London studio and photographed hundreds of aspiring models as they arrived for castings. Sometimes they came alone, sometimes with friends, sometimes with their parents. There were no elaborate sets, no stylists, and no retouching, just young people hoping that a few minutes in front of the right photographer might change their lives.

What makes Go-Sees so compelling is its honesty. Teller wasn’t interested in photographing supermodels. He was interested in the moment before fame, when confidence, nervousness, ambition, and uncertainty all existed at once. Every portrait captures the vulnerability of an industry built on first impressions.

Looking back today, the series feels like both a time capsule and a quiet critique of fashion itself. It strips away the fantasy that usually surrounds modeling and reminds us that every iconic career once began with someone standing outside a studio, waiting to be seen.

More than two decades later, Go-Sees remains one of fashion photography’s most important projects—not because it captured stars, but because it captured the people who were still dreaming of becoming one.

Credits

Photography: Juergen Teller
Project: Go-Sees
Years: 1998–1999
Location: Outside Juergen Teller’s London studio

posts.caption:
When Rihanna steps in front of Rafael Pavarotti’s camera, the result is always more than an editorial. Together with stylist Ib Kamara, they create worlds where fashion feels cinematic, surreal, and completely fearless. Every image balances bold styling with emotion, proving that the strongest editorials aren’t just about clothes, they tell a story.
For this selection, we’ve brought together some of our favorite images from Pavarotti’s two editorials featuring Rihanna: Dazed (2021) and Perfect Magazine (2024). Despite being created three years apart, they share the same unmistakable visual language, rich colors, sculptural silhouettes, dramatic beauty, and Rihanna’s unmatched ability to transform in front of the lens.
Few artists possess the range Rihanna brings to an editorial. She can disappear into a character while remaining instantly recognizable, turning every frame into something memorable. That’s what makes these stories feel timeless. They aren’t simply fashion shoots, they’re visual worlds you’ll want to revisit again and again.
Photography: Rafael Pavarotti
Talent: Rihanna
Styling: Ib Kamara
Editorials: Dazed (2021) & Perfect Magazine (2024)

posts.caption:
Not a handbag. Not sunglasses. Not jewelry. A veil !
Long before it became a runway statement, the veil carried centuries of meaning. Across cultures and civilizations, sheer fabrics draped over the face or body have symbolized everything from status, spirituality, mystery, and protection to celebration and personal identity. Whether worn in ancient Mesopotamia, across parts of the Middle East and South Asia, in European bridal traditions, or as a glamorous Hollywood accessory, the veil has never belonged to a single culture or aesthetic. Its meaning has continuously evolved, shaped by the people and societies that wore it.
Fashion eventually transformed the veil into something entirely different. Designers began treating it less as a symbol and more as a creative tool. A layer of translucent silk, chiffon, or tulle could soften a silhouette, blur the face, create movement, or introduce a sense of drama without concealing the garment itself. Rather than hiding the wearer, the veil often drew even more attention to them.
Throughout the decades, houses such as Dior, Schiaparelli, Alexander McQueen etc. have repeatedly reinterpreted the veil, using it to evoke romance, rebellion, sensuality, or surrealism. What remained constant was its ability to transform an entire look with almost no material at all.
Today, the veil is quietly making its way back into fashion—not as tradition, but as styling. A simple piece of sheer fabric can completely change the mood of an outfit, proving that sometimes the most overlooked accessory is also the most powerful.

posts.caption:
When The Great Gatsby premiered in 2013, audiences weren’t just watching a film, they were witnessing one of fashion’s most influential collaborations with cinema.
Working alongside Academy Award-winning costume designer Catherine Martin, Miuccia Prada reimagined more than 40 archival Prada and Miu Miu looks for Baz Luhrmann’s adaptation of F. Scott Fitzgerald’s classic. Rather than recreating the 1920s with historical precision, the costumes offered a contemporary interpretation of the Jazz Age, blending Prada’s modern sensibility with the glamour, excess, and fantasy that defined Gatsby’s world.￼
Covered in crystals, sequins, feathers, fringe, velvet, and silk, the garments were designed not only to look spectacular, but to move with the film’s sweeping cinematography. The result blurred the line between costume and couture, proving that fashion could become an essential part of cinematic storytelling rather than simply dressing its characters.
The collaboration had a lasting impact far beyond Hollywood. It reignited global fascination with the Roaring Twenties, fueling a wave of Art Deco-inspired fashion, embellished eveningwear, and Gatsby-themed events throughout the 2010s. At the same time, it reinforced the growing relationship between luxury fashion houses and cinema, showing that archival fashion could be reimagined as living cultural history instead of remaining confined to museums or runways.
Following the film’s release, Prada: The Great Gatsby costume exhibitions opened in New York, Tokyo, and later Shanghai, allowing the public to experience the designs as works of art in their own right. Their legacy continued years later, when several original dresses, including the now-iconic chandelier dress, were pulled from the archive for Dua Lipa’s 2021 We’re Good music video, proving that their influence extended well beyond the silver screen.
Film: The Great Gatsby
Director: Baz Luhrmann
Costume Design: Catherine Martin
Fashion Collaboration: Miuccia Prada
Fashion Houses: Prada, Miu Miu
Lead Cast: Carey Mulligan, Leonardo DiCaprio, Tobey Maguire, Joel Edgerton

posts.caption:
Long before street style became dominated by fashion weeks, luxury logos, and carefully curated content, Hel Looks was documenting something far more interesting: real people.
Founded in 2005 by Liisa Jokinen and Sampo Karjalainen, the Helsinki-based project has spent over two decades capturing the city’s unique approach to personal style. Rather than focusing on trends, Hel Looks celebrates individuality, people who dress for themselves, not for an audience.
What makes Helsinki’s style so compelling is its sense of freedom. Vintage pieces sit alongside handmade garments, borrowed clothes, thrifted finds, and unexpected combinations that would feel out of place almost anywhere else. The result is a city where fashion feels less like performance and more like self-expression.
Over the years, Hel Looks has become an archive of subcultures, creativity, and everyday originality. Each portrait is accompanied by personal reflections on clothing, inspiration, and identity, creating a portrait not only of fashion, but of the people behind it. As trends come and go, the project continues to prove that personal style will always be more interesting than following the crowd.
In a world increasingly shaped by algorithms, Hel Looks remains a reminder that the best style often happens on ordinary streets, worn by ordinary people who never intended to become fashion references in the first place.
Project: Hel Looks
Founded: 2005
Creators & Photographers: Liisa Jokinen and Sampo Karjalainen @hellooks
Location: Helsinki
Website: Hel Looks

posts.caption:
💚 The rarest luxury products aren’t always the most talked about.
Natural green diamonds are a reminder that true luxury isn’t defined by visibility—it’s defined by scarcity.
From the historic Dresden Green to the record-setting Aurora Green and a headline-making Oscars appearance in 2026, these extraordinary diamonds continue to capture the attention of collectors, auction houses and high jewellery maisons.
For retailers, the takeaway goes beyond a single gemstone.
As luxury clients increasingly seek provenance, individuality and exceptional rarity, understanding categories like natural green diamonds can elevate every high-value client conversation.
👇 If you had one extraordinary gemstone to showcase in your flagship store, which would it be—and why?
#DiamondPulse #GreenDiamonds #FancyColorDiamonds #LuxuryRetail #DiamondWorld

posts.caption:
Luxury keeps changing.
Yet pearls keep surviving.
Fashion changes.
Trends change.
Consumer preferences change.
Pearls remain.
In 2026, they’re once again at the centre of some of the jewellery world’s biggest conversations.
That should make every retailer pay attention.
Because when a material remains relevant across generations, markets and design movements, it’s no longer a trend story.
It’s a luxury story.
💬 One question:
If you were building a high jewellery collection today, would pearls be essential?
Why or why not?
Image credit @jckevents
#DiamondWorld #GemSphere #Pearls #HighJewellery #JewelleryTrends

posts.caption:
💎 THE FUTURE OF DIAMONDS, IN THEIR WORDS
An exclusive Diamond World series featuring perspectives from the official panelists of the 41st World Diamond Congress, Singapore 🇸🇬 |12–15 July 2026.
What if the industry is asking the wrong question? 💎
For years, we’ve asked:
“How do we build natural diamond demand?”
Dave Bonaparte believes the better question is:
“How do we rebuild it?”
Because demand hasn’t disappeared.
It’s shifting.
The real opportunity now lies in understanding where consumers are moving — and how the industry can evolve alongside them.
💬 Where do you think natural diamond demand is shifting today?
#DiamondWorld #NaturalDiamonds #DiamondDemand #DaveBonaparte #DiamondIndustry

posts.caption:
Paris was the first city to host a brick-and-mortar Shein store, which closed recently after being boycotted, and now the French Parliament has passed a law which will hamper the growth of ultra-fast fashion retailers in the country.
The law targets Asian e-commerce retailers like Shein (the law was reportedly nicknamed the “anti-Shein law,) and will impose fees on products based on a set of criteria designed to define and grade ultra-fast fashion. That criteria looks at the breadth of the retailers’ product assortment, the speed of new releases, and the “repairability” of garments. IE, if a company is churning out loads of cheap garments that are more costly to repair than to replace, their products will be subjected to fees.
Further, advertising will be restricted, even with regard to social media influencers. Lawmakers likened these measures to those imposed on cigarettes and alcohol, which seems an apt comparison; it is widely understood that products known to be harmful to the public shouldn’t be advertised with reckless abandon. Not unlike cigarettes, ultra-fast fashion produces significant greenhouse gas emissions and have been found to be produced with materials that are harmful to the body.
But, is there a snag in the logic? It’s important to note that the law is careful to differentiate ultra-fast fashion and just plain-old fast fashion. European-owned companies like Zara and H&M were spared, with some environmental groups and lawmakers criticizing the law, saying that it had been “watered down” to forgive the business practices of European-owned retailers. But the centrist members of parliament who introduced the bill argue that France must “start somewhere” in the fight against fast fashion.
Which, fair point. The whole planet has to start somewhere. With this new law, France is still a pioneer in pushing back against ultra-fast fashion. They’re hardly the only nation for whom fashion is big business, so here’s hoping more change is on the horizon.
posts.alt_text:
Naomi Osaka just gave Wimbledon a cultural reset.

The four-time Grand Slam champion stepped onto court yesterday in a custom look by Tokyo-based designer Hana Yagi that reimagined what Wimbledon whites can look like. The design pulled inspiration from Japanese ceremonial dress and ‘Kill Bill’ icon O-Ren Ishii, played by Lucy Liu. 

Created with vintage ceremonial garments, the outer layer was designed with Wimbledon’s nearly 150-year white dress code in mind, while embroidered cranes and cherry blossoms added texture and symbolism throughout.

Beneath it all revealed a Nike tennis dress inspired by kirigami, the Japanese art of paper cutting and folding, turning the transformation from ceremony to competition into part of the design itself.

Every detail felt intentional and before a single point was played, @naomiosaka served 👏👏

Designer: @_hanayagi
Photography: @seebasschin

#naomiosaka #wimbledon #tennis #news #fashion

posts.caption:
Have you heard? There’s a major heatwave going on in Europe.
Wednesday was the hottest day ever recorded in France, prompting the Eiffel Tower and the Louvre to restrict visiting hours.  Schools canceled classes. The Pride parade was postponed. Almost 50 people drowned while swimming to cool off. Two children died after being left in a hot vehicle. Summer celebrating in the streets was canceled as hospitals overfilled.
And yet, Paris Menswear Fashion Week pressed on. Louis Vuitton built a working tidal wave on a fake beach. Rick Owens and Dior moved their outdoor shows to early in the morning. Many of the Spring/Summer 2027 collections featured layered sweaters, coats and jackets, some made of plastic or leather, with front row attendees in brand-supplied outfits that seemed to scoff at the weather.
Looking at the content pouring out of Paris Menswear Fashion Week alongside the news of the city’s heatwave raises some concerns: Should we be holding these events while other institutions are temporarily closing? And, looking at the broader picture, is fashion using these spaces to respond to the climate crisis knowing that the industry itself is partially responsible for it?
“The calendar does not make any sense,” Jonathan Anderson said to the Associated Press, of the way the industry operates.
But is the calendar the only problem? High fashion tends to skirt the kind of criticism that fast fashion receives for contributing to climate change, but it’s time to consider the carbon footprint left behind from weeks and weeks of fashion weeks every year. Grand ephemeral set design, loads of air travel, scores of private vehicles, and accommodations for hundreds of visitors add to emissions in cities like Paris, where AC is difficult to install due to historical architecture protected by law.
Can fashion weeks as we know them stay the same and be (literally) sustainable? Or is the heatwave laying bare the truth about them: that we need to adjust our expectations, slow down, and find more responsible ways to engage with fashion?

posts.caption: Please don't forget that you can never be sure when the glasses are recording or not. And the footage is NOT secure. It gets used to train Metas AI, which means it's sent to underpaid and exploited workers in (amongst other places(?)) Kenya, who views and annotates ALL the footage to improve the systems. This means your private footage is viewed by other people. It's a safety concern with so many levels as it intersects with labour exploitation and trauma for the annotators. They don't only review personal data, emails, social security numbers, banking info etc - but also people going to the toilet, having s-x, v-olence etc... These glasses should NOT exist for SO many reasons. If you want to read more about this, please read the full story from Svenska Dagbladet (it's english) called "She Came Out of the Bathroom Naked, Employee Says" or the BBC article "Meta in row after workers who say they saw smart glasses users having s.x lose jobs". There's so many of these articles exposing how these glasses work and how messed up it is.

posts.caption:
It began when Anthony Vaccarello posted a promotional image for Saint Laurent Menswear SS27 featuring an extended-toe derby made of transparent plastic. The internet was instantly horrified by the shape, the fact that the model’s foot was visible, and the imminent sweat that would fill the shoe. Isn’t there a heatwave in France right now? The drama called to mind Chanel’s recent half-shoe debacle; Is ragebait footwear fashion’s new fetish?
Later, Madonna showed up to the front row in a fuchsia Saint Laurent mini dress. She was seated between Connor Storrie and Charli XCX, whom she promptly began smoking with (to other attendee’s chagrin.) Madonna and Storrie then had a seemingly tense conversation that has prompted Storrie stans to seek out lip readers. Do they have beef we don’t know about? If you don’t already know, Storrie’s fans are, uh, especially passionate, and track every detail of his life. But, more on that shortly.
The actual show was mostly unsurprising, leaning into Vaccarello’s trademark tailoring and juiced up 80s silhouettes. Then came Look 33: camel colored undies styled with a tan sweater and blazer, a camel choker accessory, espresso calf-socks, and the plastic derbys. Front row onlookers appeared shocked, distressed, and maybe even aroused.
But the biggest drama apparently happened before all of this, as Storrie’s superfans waited outside Hotel Le Meurice to catch a glimpse of the actor on his way to the show. Apparently, an X user who was formerly known as “clubchalamet” (now “Fan Account Storrie Glorrie”) was violently assaulted by another Storrie superfan in the crowd. In true stan fashion, it is a convoluted, verbose, and deeply ‘online’ tale that isn’t easy for outsiders to wrap their heads around. In any case some kind of physical altercation took place between the two and social media is reeling.
There is a happy ending to all this, though. And that, of course, is the Madonna-fueled Saint Laurent afterparty where she and Charli XCX danced onstage at Paradis Latin. Is a collab in the works? Hopefully it’ll be enough for us all to heal after a very hectic day of Paris Fashion Week chaos.

posts.caption:
Education time!
Whatever happened to Manish Arora’s own business is another topic for another day, but it does not erase the fact that he was the designer handpicked by PUIG to revive Paco Rabanne in 2011. His tenure as Creative Director was criminally short. Just two seasons of full-blown sci-fi madness. Like a brief summer fling that still haunts fashion’s wet dreams.
Especially that debut collection. The one Lady Gaga looked at and basically said, “Pack ‘em all.” Metallic Rhodoid dresses for the 2010-era club girlies, futuristic beading, fan-like sculptural silhouettes, and those Philip Treacy flying saucer hats. And y’all thought avant-garde started with Iris van Herpen lol
In barely a year, he generated global press, dressed an enviable roster of clients, delivered commercially successful accessories, and put the house back into the conversation. Then he was asked to leave. “Parted ways” as the SPAM girlies love to write in their releases.
Looking back now, it feels strangely familiar. Once again we’re watching creative directors being judged after a season or two, shuffled from house to house before a vision has the chance to settle. We call it the creative director carousel today, but Manish was living it more than 15 years ago. This isn’t about diminishing Julien Dossena’s extraordinary 16-year tenure at Rabanne. Quite the opposite. His legacy proves exactly what time can do for a designer and a house. Imagine if Manish had been afforded the same runway. Not one year, but five. Fashion history might have looked very different

posts.caption:
Apparently the streets, the group chats, and the SPAM girilies are all saying Instagram now lets you REPLY to comments with photos from your camera roll? Excuse?
What a remarkable moment in the history of civilisation. We invented language, built the internet, AI, and have finally arrived at the ability to leave completely unsolicited images under a stranger’s comment. A blurry screenshot. A reaction meme from 2018. Your neighbour’s cat. A grainy airport paparazzi shot. The possibilities are genuinely terrifying.
Let’s test this little social experiment. If it’s real, drop your favourite WhatsApp sticker in the comments. I need to witness this technological leap with my own eyeS!!
posts.alt_text:
Fatafat June recap 🌶️⭐️🫢

	⁃	Pride month is over. Back to hetero land.
	⁃	Obsession has kicked every movie out of the box office race. 
	⁃	Menswear fashion week is up! Indian models >>
	⁃	Diptyque’s kachua chaap coil has the entire south and south east Asia quaking.
	⁃	The heatwave has sent everyone to their melting point. Euro-summer what?
	⁃	Cocktail 2 promoted. Cocktail 2 released. Cocktail 2 gone.
	⁃	Zendaya’s press lewks are not giving this time.
	⁃	An AI song has gone more viral than all these regurgitated remakes.
	⁃	Make one wish on the “one wish willow” for July, chalo.

posts.caption:
@c.syresmith has us (and y’all, the community) on a #DSexclu tour of his @christianlouboutin exhibition. It’s giving archaeological, henny 🔴💅
It’s the classic dystopian tale. The world as we know it has been abandoned. Humanity has vanished. Civilisation has crumbled. And all that remains are RED HOT Louboutins. Foam shoes, razor-sharp patent boots, and leather quiver-and-arrow bags (hello, Katniss Everdeen) now sit like prehistoric artefacts, clues to a forgotten land and its impossibly fashionable inhabitants. Grab your fossil brushes and shovels, girlies. It’s time to start digging for those RED BOTTOMS. 🔴👀💅

#ELLEDigitalCoverStar: There is a version of Kirandeep Chahal’s (@kirandeep_chahal) backstory that will hit close to home for many Indian women. To come from a family that cannot comprehend the desire for a life earned and lived on your own terms. To be expected to get married young, start a family, and follow a pre-written narrative folded neatly into the same shape it has always taken. Then there is the version she followed instead, one that eventually led her to becoming a familiar fixture on the runways of Dior, Dries Van Noten and Rick Owens, among other names that make fashion disciples starstruck.
Head to the 🔗 in bio to read the cover story.

posts.caption:
#ELLECoverStar: There is a version of Zinnia Kumar (now Rose) (@zinniakumar) that fashion understands easily. The model. An image that moves seamlessly between Paris, New York, and wherever the industry gathers next.
It is also the least interesting version of her.
We’re at Le Bristol in Paris and the day carries all the familiar markers of a luxury cover shoot. Controlled light. Precise framing. Cartier anchoring the narrative. But Rose doesn’t perform that script in the expected way. She arrives ready, slips into each look without negotiation, barely checks the monitor. There is no sense of overworking the image, only an instinctive understanding of how to move within it. Head to the 🔗 in bio to read our conversation with the cover star.
On @zinniakumar: Clash de Cartier earrings in rose gold and green dyed agate, Clash de Cartier necklace in rose gold, Clash de Cartier ring in rose gold and chalcedony, Clash De Cartier ring in rose gold and onyx, and Clash de Cartier ring in rose gold and red dyed agate, all by @cartier. Textured draped top paired and draped trousers both by @newbottega
Editorial Director and words by: Ainee Nizami Ahmedi (@aineenizamiahmedi)
Photographer: Noémi Ottilia Szabo (@noemiottiliaszabo)
Stylist: Rhia Kapoor (@rhiakapoor)

posts.caption:
#ELLECulture: The most exciting brands today are building communities before they become household names. They understand aesthetics, but they also back it with products that people genuinely want to use, gift and recommend. Whether it is a beautifully designed can of water, a perfume inspired by Indian fields or a box of mithai that earns a permanent spot on your coffee table, these homegrown labels prove that some of the country's most interesting ideas are coming from categories we usually overlook.  Head to the 🔗 in bio to discover the latest artisanal brands.
Words by: @anamm__24
[ homegrown brands, lifestyle ]

posts.caption:
#ELLECoverStar: Few cityscapes are as instantly recognisable as Victoria Harbour, where Hong Kong's glittering skyline rises dramatically from the water. It's the perfect backdrop for our latest cover shoot, with Jacqueline Fernandez (@jacquelienefernandez) bringing her effortless charisma to one of the city's most iconic landmarks. As the light shifts across the harbour, so does the mood, proving that true magnetism doesn't always demand attention—sometimes, it's simply felt. Read our exclusive interview with the cover star at the link in bio.
On @jacquelienefernandez: Corset Octopus and Bell pants, both by @_demo_official. Crater ring and Wave amethyst ring, both by @figmenterie. Moo mules by @shopceecee___

posts.caption:
Presenting our first of four Summer Issue cover stars, @annehathaway.
Due to filming and release delays, Hathaway now has five movies premiering within six months. Her insanely busy year continues with her starring role as Penelope in @theodysseymovie. (To which she says: “It’s crazy to have four years of work coming out in six months.”) After weathering the storms of Hollywood, the in-demand star is finally collecting her flowers—and stopping to smell them. “If I’m not enjoying it, then really, really what’s wrong with me?” she tells ELLE. “If I can’t have a positive outlook on it when I’m having such a fortunate ride, then it would be wasted on me.”
For ELLE’s Summer 2026 issue, Hathaway reflects on how she’s grown since the early days of her career, reuniting with Christopher Nolan after 12 years, and navigating online criticism: “We’re at a time when people feel very confident in assuming what they think is fact.” Read the full cover story at the link in bio.
ELLE: @elleusa
Editor-in-chief: Nina Garcia @ninagarcia
Photographer: Norman Jean Roy @normanjeanroy
Stylist: Law Roach @luxurylaw
Writer: Kayla Webley Adler @kaylaw
Hair: Orlando Pita at Orlo Salon @hairbyorlandopita @orlosalon
Makeup: Fulvia Farolfi at MA+ Group @fulviafarolfi @maworldgroup
Manicure: Deborah Lippmann for Deborah Lippmann @thelippmannofficial @thedeborahlippmann
Set design: Marla Weinhoff at 11th House Agency @marlaweinhoff @11thhouseagency
Produced by Alexey Galetskiy Productions @alexeyg
posts.alt_text:
Not sure if you’ve noticed, but recently there has been a strange phenomenon in grocery stores everywhere: sardines are flying off the shelves. At the onset of the trend a few months ago, sardines had gone so viral that Trader Joe’s even implemented a two-tin-per-customer limit for a short period of time. Online, conversations stir about how locations nationwide were facing a shortage. There's even been talk of locations taking sardine tins off the shelves and gatekeeping them at the front desk.

But what many people don't know is that it's all in the name of beauty. Now, sardines are nothing new, but if you've been scrolling on BeautyTok lately, there's a good chance your timeline has been a bit fishy. Over the last few weeks, influencers—and the influenced—have been raving about sardines and the various recipes and health benefits that the tiny tinned fish can produce. At the link in bio, ELLE chatted with skin care experts and social media's Sardine Queen, @tonibravo, to get some answers.

Video: TikTok/bonitravo

posts.caption: @annehathaway’s maternity style is red-hot. Styled by @erinwalshstyle, Hathaway wore the piece backward, spotlighting her @bvlgari choker.

For ELLE’s Summer 2026 issue, Hathaway reflects on how she’s grown since the early days of her career, reuniting with Christopher Nolan after 12 years, and navigating online criticism: “We’re at a time when people feel very confident in assuming what they think is fact.” Read the full cover story at the link in bio.

posts.caption:
Get ready for a dance-filled summer, thanks to @chanelofficial. Dance has always been integral to the maison; after all, founder Gabrielle Chanel was a close friend and patron of Serge Diaghilev, later designing costumes for several Ballets Russes productions.
More than a century later, the fashion house is reaffirming its commitment to the art form through the BAAND Together Dance Festival, a weeklong celebration of dance that brings together five of New York City’s leading companies—@ballethispanico, @alvinailey, @abtofficial, @nycballet and @dancetheatreofharlem—for performances on one stage at @lincolncenter. #BAANDTogether offers the rare chance to experience programming curated collectively by the artistic directors of each company, including repertory favorites.
Spotlighting the power of collaboration and expanding access to world-class dance, the festival opens July 28 and choose-what-you-pay tickets are currently available. Tap the link in bio for all the details.
Photos: @darrengwynnstudio

posts.caption:
When people throw out the phrase la dolce vita, they’re thinking about Capri. The tiny (four-square-mile!) limestone island has changed little since the 1960s, when the small fishing village became a vacation destination for writers, actors, and the fashion elite. In Capri, everything is within walking distance. The days are simple ones, spent swimming in the Tyrrhenian Sea, gazing at the faraglioni rock formations, and eating once-in-a-lifetime meals. Capri is a very social place, and the best part is the bustling streets full of little shops and amazing restaurants—and how you can run into friends you haven’t seen for ages.
For this month’s Insider Guide, I recruited recommendations from my dear friends Michael Kors and journalist @thealinacho, both frequent visitors to Capri. Subscribe at the link in bio for our collective guide to the island’s highlights, from hotels and restaurants to niche shops and can’t-miss beach clubs.
posts.alt_text:
Presenting the next of four Summer Issue cover stars, @zendaya.

On the cusp of 30, the actress is in the midst of her busiest season yet, with ‘The Drama’ and Euphoria behind her and @theodysseymovie and ‘Spider-Man: Brand New Day’ ahead—followed by ‘Dune: Part Three’ in December. “It’s going to be a long year, but what’s great is that I’m really proud of everything. I just really love my job,” she tells ELLE.

The Emmy winner stars as Athena, the goddess of wisdom, in Christopher Nolan’s summer blockbuster. “I think I do have wisdom to offer,” she says, though she still feels like a kid as she prepares to leave her 20s behind: “Like, what the fuck, I’m about to be 30? I still feel like a child inside.”

For ELLE’s Summer 2026 issue, Zendaya opens up about working with the legendary director for the first time, the pride she felt watching her “best friend” @tomholland2013 on set, and the need for empathy in today’s political climate: “How much we lean into dehumanizing other people—that breaks my heart.” Read the full interview at the link in bio.

posts.caption:
When it comes to court style, no one serves like @serenawilliams.
In the early 2000s, while collecting titles at the US Open and Wimbledon, it became clear that Williams’s eye for style would become as much a part of her legacy as her dominance on the court. “Style was always part of how Serena showed up,” says Williams’s longtime stylist, @keshamcleod. “For a long time, tennis was only about the game. With Serena, what she wore on court was part of who she was—not something added on. Fashion and competition were never two different things for her.”
From dramatic walk-on capes to custom jackets and floor-sweeping trains, the performative element of the athlete’s outfits inspired the grand entrances embraced by a new generation of tennis stars, including @naomiosaka and @cocogauff. But however stylish her ensembles were, they always prioritized performance and function.
As the tennis icon returns to Wimbledon, revisit what makes her fashion sense one of one at the link in bio.

posts.caption:
Wimbledon is off to a thrilling start. Jannik Sinner (@janniksin) battled past Miomir Kecmanović (@miomirk) in a gripping five-setter (4-6, 6-3, 6-7, 6-2, 6-3), overcoming 52 unforced errors to keep his title defence alive. Meanwhile, Novak Djokovic (@djokernole) extended his remarkable 21-0 first-round Wimbledon record, digging deep to beat Wu Yibing 6-4, 5-7, 6-4, 6-4. With David Beckham (@davidbeckham), Bad Bunny (@badbunnypr), and Niall Horan (@niallhoran) in the stands, Centre Court delivered on day one.

posts.caption:
“Hi Shakaila we’re xyz producers and we’d love to make a tv show with you” I’ve had a handful of those conversations over the years and they quickly go from 🤩 to 🫩
I used to feel disheartened until I looked around and saw so many examples of people not waiting for permission and instead deciding to get up and make shit happen! So that’s what I decided to do, turn this decades long dream into a reality! I pulled in every favour I had! Turned my friends into producers and show runners, begged and borrowed and you know what, it turned out pretty damn good if I do say so myself 😅
This black history month especially, I’m inspired by the talented Black British women who have shown me that it’s possible to make a mark on the small screen no matter the limitations!
I absolutely loved makeover shows growing up! But as I got older I saw how they were more brutal than beautiful!
Today, fashion psychology shows us just how deeply our clothes impact our mental health, identity, and confidence.
That’s why I’m so excited to share The Wearapy Show my new makeover series that blends fashion psychology, emotional healing and real style transformations.
We help everyday people reclaim their wardrobes after major life shifts: grief, motherhood, identity struggles, and more.
With a team of experts and no judgment, just science-backed guidance and love.
Episodes 1 and 2 launch 26th October on the Fashion is Psychology YouTube Channel. Subscribe now and follow the journey.
#bhm #wearapy #fashionpsychology #makeover
posts.alt_text:
💕💚After I went on Lorraine last year I made a silent promise to myself that I would be back at the ITV studios and I had my eyes set on @thismorning. I can’t believe just a few months later, this promise became a reality.

Of course I was low key stressing 😅😅 but there was absolutely zero reason to be nervous because the entire (and I’m not exaggerating) team were so warm and lovely that I felt like I was hanging with friends all day. 

Firstly thank you for everyone that tuned in and sent good luck vibes🍀💖! A huge shout out to @lilaflintroberts @sarah_barlow  and @amberstyledit for the opportunity to shine a light on #fashionpsychology and for allowing me to flex my styling skills. 

💃🏾🔥I couldn’t have asked for better models to showcase those amazing looks. @kate_mitchell78 @jazzifizzle53 and @misskspence you KILLT IT 

And last but certainly not least the pros @hollywilloughby and @alisonhammond55 thank you for making me feel so comfortable, it was an honour to watch you both work! 

Stay tuned for a reel on all of the BTS goss but for now I’m in Estonia about to speak on the biggest stage I have so far. Wish me luck! 🍀💖 

Dress: @fumithelabel 
Shoes: @asos 

#Tv #fashionpsychology #press #itv #thismorning

posts.caption: That’s why I like the 24h rule. Unless I’ve been actively searching for something already, I wait 24h with something in my basket. 1) I might get a voucher code for ‘abandoning my cart’. But 2) it stops me impulse buying when I’m feeling stressed. It forces me to reflect if I actually want that piece in my wardrobe. And 80% of the time when I’m impulse shopping, it’s ultimately a no!

Same as the Fendi Baguette : the bag was created in 1997, the tv show aired in 1998, and the Baguette is introduced in season 3, episode 17, so at least in 2000/2001… How can it be the it-bag of the 1990s ?

posts.caption:
“You know, there comes a time at a very crucial point in your life when you become absolutely fearless and just want to work without having anything else on your mind. The only thing I think about is whether I will love doing what I’ve just committed to. Of course, you want the outcome to be proportionate to your passion, but I also know it is not in my hands. What is in my control is to make sure that whatever I say ‘yes’ to, I’m happy to do, to enjoy it thoroughly.”

posts.caption:
#FeminaBusiness: Juhi Chawla’s (@iamjuhichawla) career is often remembered through the lens of cinema, but her entrepreneurial journey tells an equally compelling story.
From co-founding Kolkata Knight Riders, now one of the world’s most valuable cricket franchises; to expanding her presence across business and investments, she has quietly built a legacy that extends well beyond the screen.
Her journey reflects the power of reinvention: transforming influence into enterprise, balancing creativity with strategy, and proving that long-term success comes from building businesses that endure.
#Femina #JuhiChawla #BusinessJourney #Entrepreneurship

posts.caption:
#FeminaTrending: Long before curated feeds, viral reels, and algorithm-approved content, there were platforms that defined an era of the internet. We personalized MySpace profiles, collected Orkut testimonials, laughed through endless Vine loops, and found communities on Tumblr: each app capturing a unique moment in digital culture.
Some evolved, others quietly disappeared, but their influence lives on in the way we create, connect, and communicate today.
This World Social Media Day, we’re revisiting the platforms that shaped our online memories and sparked trends long before they became mainstream.
#Femina #WorldSocialMediaDay #DigitalCulture #InternetNostalgia

posts.caption:
HUGE NEWS: Good On You has officially rated over 7,000 brands 🎉 This is a major accomplishment, as even more brands—from the small and indie through the large and luxury—are in our Directory.⁠
⁠
What goes into ratings? 🤔 A lot, actually: we have a rigorous, comprehensive system analysing up to 1,000 data points across 100 key issues to arrive at our lowest score (“We Avoid”) to our highest score (“Great”). Ratings are based solely on a brand’s transparent, public reporting that we pair with credible third-party indices, certifications, and standards.⁠
⁠
How do we select brands to rate? Well…with your help 🥹 Many of our ratings come through community requests, shared in comments and messages, which helps us understand which brands to prioritise to meet this moment. Additionally, our retail and ecommerce partners along with brands request ratings too, which is a key means to apply change to the industry from within.⁠
⁠
Another item to celebrate here? Of these rated brands, 1,000 have received our two highest scores, “Good” and “Great”. This is very exciting news, representative of how more brands are doing considering their impact on workers, the planet, and animals. (Even if that also means…6000 brands have a lot more to do to reduce their impact.)⁠
⁠
Here’s to continuing to keep people like you informed while supporting more sustainable brands that are making the difference! Cheers 🥂 Explore our 7,000 ratings at the link too ✨⁠
⁠
#SustainableFashion #FastFashion #ConsciousConsumer #EthicalConsumer #SustainableStyle #ConsciousStyle #GoodOnYou #GoodOnYouApp #EthicalFashion⁠
posts.alt_text:
Meet the top rated brands of 2025 🎊⁠
⁠
In the past year, we rated more than 1500 brands and are proud to share the ten that received our highest marks, illustrating a commitment to more sustainable practices that protect people, the planet, and animals 💚⁠
⁠
What’s a theme that unites these brands? 👀 Outside of a commitment to reducing their impact, these brands are all small and independent, a reminder of where to look in the industry for leadership and innovation. If you’re looking for a reminder to support small brands this year, this is it.⁠
⁠
Cheers to all these brands, along with all the brands that were rated “Good” and “Great” in the past year! Here’s to continuing to make a difference and for championing a better approach to what we wear. Find the full list at the link—and share which brands you’re hoping to see rated in 2026 in the comments 💬⁠
⁠
#SustainableFashion #FastFashion #SustainableBeauty #EcoBeauty #Beauty #BeautyBrand #ConsciousConsumer #EthicalConsumer #GoodOnYou #GoodOnYouApp #EthicalFashion

posts.caption:
What can be accomplished in ten years?⁠
⁠
A decade may seem short but, in reflecting on all the work we’ve done in the past decade, we’re in awe of everything that has transpired—and are grateful for the hard work of the current and former team members along with you.⁠
⁠
Whether you’ve shared our posts or have been a partner in this business, the work of creating a more sustainable future isn’t done alone but in partnership with others. These accomplishments are an example of what happens when likeminded people come together in the hopes of a better world 🌍🌎🌏⁠
⁠
Swipe to learn about ten things Good On You has accomplished in the past ten years. Share how you think the sustainable fashion and beauty industry have shifted in the past decade 💚⁠
⁠

posts.caption:
As this #Pride month comes to a close, we’re reflecting on how inclusivity and sustainability work together 💚⁠
⁠
Charlie McEvoy, founder of @plantfacedclothing, knows this very well—and has folded this into their approach to running their brand via gender neutrality. “Gender neutral clothing and sustainability are closely connected because both encourage a more thoughtful approach to consumption,” McEvoy explained. “When garments are designed to be worn by anyone, they often have a broader appeal and a longer lifespan. They can be shared between partners, friends, or family members, and are less likely to become obsolete because of changing trends or gender norms.”⁠
⁠
This relates to being more thoughtful with your clothing, to think more critically about what one puts on the body, where it came from, and what your relationship with the garment may be. “Sustainable fashion is about creating clothing that people genuinely connect with and want to wear again and again,” they said. “By focusing on timeless designs, inclusive fits, and messages that resonate across different communities, we're aiming to create pieces that stay in wardrobes longer and ultimately reduce unnecessary consumption.”⁠
⁠
Sustainability is as about taking a holistic and intersectional approach to the items in your life, to reduce harm to people, planet, and animals 🌱 If you’re interested in exploring more gender neutral and unisex clothing from brands like Plant Faced, visit the link for a full list ↑⁠
⁠
#Sustainability #GenderNeutral #LGBT #SustainableFashion #Fashion

posts.caption:
Presenting one of our three Summer issue covers with the leading men of @theodysseymovie: Matt Damon as Odysseus.
A Hollywood veteran of nearly five decades now, Damon has worked on approximately 80 film sets. And yet, as he told GQ’s @zachbaron, “Every single location on this movie would’ve been the hardest location on any other movie I’ve ever done… Every time we’d go somewhere, we’d be like, ‘Well, Iceland will be easier.’ And then it’s raining sideways and it’s fucking freezing. Iceland was like, ‘Yeah, easy? Hey, hold my beer.’”
For Damon, though, the challenge was part of what made the project so special: “All of the things that might have been difficult for me at an earlier point in my life weren’t difficult. They became fun. Like being wet and cold was not a hardship. It was actually something I felt really lucky to be experiencing. It felt temporary and like a gift.”
Damon unwraps the ‘gift’ that was making The Odyssey, alongside his co-stars Robert Pattinson and Tom Holland, and director Christopher Nolan, for GQ’s summer issue cover story at the 🔗 in bio.
Story by @zachbaron
Photographs by @alexprager
Styled by @georgecortina
Hair by @teddycharleshair at Nevermind
Skin by @jostrettell at Walter Schupfer Management
Set design by @joneslikeindiana
Tailoring by Yelena Travkina and Susanna Badalyan
Key Gaffer by @mick411
Key Grip by @clayton_n_iii
Retouching by @bermudezstudio
Modeling by Kara Smatt
Produced by @campproductions
posts.alt_text:
Presenting one of our three Summer issue covers with the leading men of @theodysseymovie: Robert Pattinson as Antinous. 

Pattinson’s part in The Odyssey is not big—he plays the cockiest of the many suitors who have installed themselves at Ithaca, seeking the hand of Penelope (played by Anne Hathaway) while Matt Damon’s Odysseus is away. “My character’s a little like James Woods in Casino. That was my inspiration for it,” Pattinson said. “He’s like a little sleazy. I kept saying at my costume fitting, ‘I really want to have leopard underpants.’”

Pattinson talks to GQ’s @zachbaron about teaming up with Nolan again for The Odyssey, alongside co-stars Matt Damon and Tom Holland, at the 🔗 in bio.

Story by @zachbaron
Photographs by @alexprager
Styled by @georgecortina
Hair by @teddycharleshair at Nevermind
Skin by @jostrettell at Walter Schupfer Management
Set design by @joneslikeindiana
Tailoring by Yelena Travkina and Susanna Badalyan
Key Gaffer by @mick411
Key Grip by @clayton_n_iii
Retouching by @bermudezstudio
Modeling by Kara Smatt
Produced by @campproductions

posts.caption:
Presenting one of our three Summer issue covers with the leading men of @theodysseymovie: Tom Holland as Telemachus.
When Christopher Nolan first offered Holland a part in The Odyssey, the production dates were identical to those of Spider-Man: Brand New Day (and Holland’s commitments to the Spider-Man franchise are ironclad). “I said to Chris, like, ‘Look, I want to do this movie, but if I’m going to do it, I’m going to have to call Sony and have a very uncomfortable conversation,’ ” Holland said. Then he immediately had a very uncomfortable conversation.
“Chris has that reputation of ‘This movie isn’t going to go five months over, and we aren’t actually going to lose Tom for two years,’ ” Holland said. “Any other director, it might have been a slightly different conversation.” The Odyssey started on the day Nolan said it would and finished nine days early.
For our summer cover story, Holland talks about coming of age on set, getting to star alongside his partner on another project, and how he’s approaching the future of his career at the 🔗 in bio.
Story by @zachbaron
Photographs by @alexprager
Styled by @georgecortina
Hair by @teddycharleshair at Nevermind
Skin by @jostrettell at Walter Schupfer Management
Set design by @joneslikeindiana
Tailoring by Yelena Travkina and Susanna Badalyan
Key Gaffer by @mick411
Key Grip by @clayton_n_iii
Retouching by @bermudezstudio
Modeling by Kara Smatt
Produced by @campproductions
posts.alt_text:
Presenting one of our three Summer issue covers with the leading men of @theodysseymovie: Matt Damon as Odysseus. 

A Hollywood veteran of nearly five decades now, Damon has worked on approximately 80 film sets. And yet, as he told GQ’s @zachbaron, “Every single location on this movie would’ve been the hardest location on any other movie I’ve ever done… Every time we’d go somewhere, we’d be like, ‘Well, Iceland will be easier.’ And then it’s raining sideways and it’s fucking freezing. Iceland was like, ‘Yeah, easy? Hey, hold my beer.’”

For Damon, though, the challenge was part of what made the project so special: “All of the things that might have been difficult for me at an earlier point in my life weren’t difficult. They became fun. Like being wet and cold was not a hardship. It was actually something I felt really lucky to be experiencing. It felt temporary and like a gift.”

Damon unwraps the ‘gift’ that was making The Odyssey, alongside his co-stars Robert Pattinson and Tom Holland, and director Christopher Nolan, for GQ’s summer issue cover story at the 🔗 in bio.

posts.caption:
Much like a learner driver, or a toddler in their father’s shoes, when a cigarette is in the hands of a non-smoker something just feels a bit… off. There’s something about the mechanics of rolling, lighting, ashing and holding that leaves nowhere for a novice to hide.
It’s something that appears to be lost on a swathe of celebrities who have, almost 20 years after the indoor smoking ban, decided that smoking is cool again – or rather that smoking looks cool again.
Just last week, intergenerational arbiters of cool Charli XCX and Madonna shared Djarum Black cloves on the front row of the Saint Laurent menswear show in Paris. It was a moment conceivably designed for social media, but it didn’t exactly land well - dubbed too ‘performantive’ and ‘cringe’ to be cool.
Between Hailey Bieber, Addison Rae and Kylie Jenner, all of whom have used cigarettes as props recently, aesthetic nicotine consumption is having something of a renaissance. But is doing something because it looks cool ever really cool? TAP the link in the bio as Grazia’s @nikpeach weighs in.

posts.caption:
The adidas Stan Smith has had more than one life. First conceived as a tennis shoe in 1963 and officially renamed in 1973, it reached fashion icon status in 2012 when Phoebe Philo, then creative director of Céline, took her runway bow wearing the humble white trainer.
Fast forward to 2026, and the classic silhouette is getting another revival. This time, courtesy of American designer Willy Chavarria, who unveiled his reimagining of the Stan Smith on the runway during Paris Fashion Week Men’s.
Proof that true classics never stay on the sidelines for long. Tap the link in bio to read more.
